text: Hello, my name is Dr. Selva. I'm a consultant obstetrician and gynecologist at Makota Medical Center, Malacca, Malaysia. In this video, I will describe how I managed to perform a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a two point two five kilogram uterus by performing a subtotal hysterectomy first.

Madam YSM, a fifty-two year old lady, para four, who presented with irregular menses and an abdominal mass. She was otherwise asymptomatic. Examination revealed a twenty-four week size uterus which was mobile. Ultrasound showed a large uterine fibroid measuring twenty by thirteen centimeters.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic hysterectomy using 3D laparoscopy on a large uterus. Adhesiolysis is conducted to separate the omentum and intestines from the left ovary. The left ovary and the left side of the pouch of Douglas are visible, while the right ovary is obscured due to its high position. The procedure begins with coagulating and cutting the left round ligament using the Erbie bisect, followed by opening the peritoneum anteriorly up to the bladder.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision to the right side, coagulating and cutting the right round ligament using surgical instruments.

The surgeon separates the bladder from the cervix, then elevates the left ovary to coagulate and cut the left infundibulopelvic ligament. The incision is extended to the ascending branch of the left uterine artery, and the bladder is further released from the cervix.

The surgeon sutures the ascending branch of the uterine artery at two points using a cinch knot to ligate it, followed by a second suture to prevent backflow bleeding. Due to the uterine fibroid's position on the right side, the surgeon creates a window in the right broad ligament as the right infundibulopelvic ligament is inaccessible.

The surgeon accesses the ascending branch of the right uterine artery through a window in the right broad ligament. The incision is extended to clearly visualize the ascending branch of the right uterine vessels, which is then sutured.

The surgeon places a second suture distal to the first on the ascending branch of the right uterine artery, which is then coagulated and cut between the two sutures. The right ovary, located high up, remains inaccessible for the right infundibulopelvic ligament. The suture is accidentally coagulated and comes off, prompting the placement of another suture. The ascending branch of the right uterine artery is coagulated again and cut. Due to the large uterus and adhesions at the left uterosacral ligament, a subtotal hysterectomy is performed first. Scissors are used to cut the cervix for better control, with bleeding at the right angle managed by grasping and coagulating the area to detach the cervix from the uterus.

The surgeon opens the vagina using a vaginal tube to delineate the cervix. The left cardinal ligament is coagulated and cut, followed by an extension of the incision to the vaginal opening. The thickened left utero sacral ligament is excised with difficulty. The anterior incision is extended to the right side, where the right cardinal ligament is coagulated and cut. The incision is further extended, detaching the cervix from the vagina, and the cervix is removed. The right infundibulopelvic ligament is isolated, coagulated, and cut.

The surgeon cuts the enlarged uterus into two halves, followed by further division of each half to facilitate vaginal morcellation. The uterus and fibroids are then removed through the vaginal opening.
The surgeon sutures the vaginal wad using interrupted sutures with polyglycine one sutures, ensuring closure of the vaginal opening.

This is how the pelvis looked at the end of the surgery. The specimen weighed two point two five kilograms. I was fortunate that the fibroid was fundal and so both the ascending branches of the uterine arteries were accessible for ligation and suture. I had no need for identifying and suturing the uterine arteries at its origin. Once that was done, I found it easier to do a subtotal hysterectomy first and then taking out the cervix separately. There was thickening of the left utero sacral ligaments and it would have been difficult to take the cervix out with the body of the uterus. I took longer to remove the specimen. It was not possible to do a vaginal morselation on this uterus as it was globularly enlarged. It was difficult to pull it down into the vagina. I decided to cut it abdominally with a knife first and then remove the uterus vaginally. In this way I shortened the time of vaginal morselation. The surgery was completed in two hours forty minutes with half the time spent on removal of the specimen. Another option to remove such big uterus is to perform a mini laparotomy. Since she was asymptomatic and the fibroid was fundal, I decided to proceed with the TLH. However, if the fibroid was intramural and she had heavy menses, I would have opted to shrink the fibroid with GnRH analog first for three months before proceeding to the TLH. In this way, the fibroid and uterus would have been smaller and surgery would have been easier. Another option in difficult situations with large fibroids is to do a myomectomy first and then proceeding to the hysterectomy. This will cause more bleeding during the surgery.

Thank you for watching this video. Please click like if you have enjoyed this video. Please subscribe to this channel to get more videos on women's health and tips and tricks in laparoscopic surgery. Bye.
